coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots nanoparticles for detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Alhokbany, Norah
Althagafi, Hebah
Ahmed, Jahangeer
Alshehri, Saad M.
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal method has been employed for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) nanoparticles at 180 °C. CDs were characterized by powder x-ray diffraction (XRD), FT-IR, electron microscopy, thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA), UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. XRD results reveal the formation of CDs. Electron microscopic studies show the spherical shape of CDs without apparent aggregation. Optical absorption measurements indicate a direct bandgap of 4.6ev. The electrochemical studies were conducted for the detection of ascorbic acid (A.A.) using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanoparticles
Hydrothermal
Fluorescence
Sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis, (b) fluorescence, and (c) TGA analysis of CDs.
(a) XRD, (b) FESEM (scale bare = 100 nm), (c) Particle size histogram, and (d) TEM analysis of CDs (scale bare = 50 nm).
Cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE and CDs/GCE in A.A. on the CQDs/GCE at different volume (60, 80, 100,and 120 µl) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.6) (b) DPV response of bare GCE and CQDs/GCE.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots nanoparticles for detection of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal method has been employed for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) nanoparticles at 180 °C. CDs were characterized by powder x-ray diffraction (XRD), FT-IR, electron microscopy, thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA), UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. XRD results reveal the formation of CDs. Electron microscopic studies show the spherical shape of CDs without apparent aggregation. Optical absorption measurements indicate a direct bandgap of 4.6ev. The electrochemical studies were conducted for the detection of ascorbic acid (A.A.) using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanoparticles (NPs), especially spherical carbon dots (CDs)with size less than 10 nm, are significant in biomedical and materials sciences because of low toxicity, chemical stability, biocompatibility, solubility, ease of functionalization and strong fluorescence [1]. Because of their smaller size compared to the bulk material, QDs have intriguing physical properties. The optical property is one of the significant properties to make CDs admirable materials in biomedical applications. Also, the nano-sized and cellular compatibility of CDs permit them to use in diagnostics, drug delivery, therapeutics, bio-imaging, and electrochemical bio-sensing [2]. Various synthetic techniques including hydrothermal, microwave, thermal decomposition, polymerization, carbonization reactions etc have been used to obtained the nano-sized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [3]. The hydrothermal method is a one-step, low-cost, environmentally friendly process with low photo-bleaching resistance and produces nearly uniform-sized CQDs with high luminescence [4]. Ascorbic acid (A.A.) has a significant role in the human body as a nutrient and antioxidant. It enters many life processes including cell division, melanogenesis inhibition, iron absorption, metabolism, and acceleration of collagen synthesis, [5]. It cannot be synthesized by humans themselves while it can be found in food and drugs [6]. But the abnormal concentration of A.A. could lead to diseases such as mental and digestive disorder, cancer, AIDS etc [7]. In this paper, carbon dots (CDs) were produced from the hydrothermal method at 180 °C. The structure and morphology were investigated by UV–visible, FT-IR, XRD, TGA, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). CDs were electro-polymerized on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to form an electrochemical sensor for the sensing of ascorbic acid (A.A.). The electrochemical behavior of the prepared sensor was examined by CV and DPV measurements.

Tri sodium citrate from VWR BDH, and ammonium hydrogen carbonate (NH4HCO3) from Lobachemie were purchased and used as received. A solution of sodium citrate (0.2 g), NH4HCO3 (1.5 g) with 10 ml deionized water stirred for 15 min followed by sonication for 15 min. After that, the resulting mixture was filtered and then sealed into a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave. Thereafter, the autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 6 h and then cooled at room temperature. Finally, a light brown colored sample was obtained.

The absorption spectra of the CDs were determined using a U.V. spectrophotometer (UV-1800) SHIMADZU. FTIR data was recorded on PerkinElmer instrument in the wavelength range from 350 to 4400 cm−1. XRD data was recorded on multipurpose x-ray diffraction system Rigaku in the 2θ range of 20°–80°. Thermogravimetric analysis was conducted on (PYRIS 1 TGA) PERKIN ELMER. The morphological analysis of the particles was obtained by SEM (JEOL7610F) and TEM (JEM-1400).

In this study, the electrochemical properties were tested for CQD by the CV and DPV techniques. The electrochemical measurements for A.A. sensing were conducted with CHI-660E electrochemical workstation using a cell of three electrodes: the working electrode the active materials loading on carbon glass, the reference electrode (Ag/AgCl) and Pt wire as counter electrode. The cyclic voltammetry was applied in the voltage window (-1 to + 1) V with a scan rate of 0.05 V/s. The differential pulse voltammetry was applied in the voltage window (-1 to + 1 V) for CQD.

For CV and DPV measurements, the cathodes were fabricated with drops of CQD sample with a few drops of Nafion as a binder to form a homogeneous surface and promote the coalescence of this mixture on the surface of the electrode. After that, the ultrasonication was used for 15 min. A drop of homogeneous suspensions was placed on the surface of GCE followed by drying in air at ambient temperature to generate a GCE-modified CQD electrode for electrochemical measurements.

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence emission studies. UV–vis spectrum show two absorption peaks at short wavelengths (Fig. 1 a) because CDs are photon-harvesting agents. The peak at ∼ 220 nm corresponds to the π–π* transition of sp2 conjugated carbon C = C and C-C, which cannot produce an observable fluorescence signal, however a shoulder ∼ 344 nm is associated with the n–π* of C = O bonds or C = OH in sp3 [9,10]. The bandgap calculations were performed using the (Eq. (1)) of the energy gap (Eg) of the CDs using the relation where c is the velocity of light, h is the Planck constant, λ is the wavelength of light lasts in supporting Table 1S.
(1)
Eg =
hc
λ

The estimated average band gap energy result is 4.6 eV. Fluorescence measurement was conducted for further investigation of the optical properties of the CDs (Fig. 1b). The emission wavelength of CDs was observed at ∼ 544 nm. The broad fluorescence can be attributed to the extrinsic broadening of the functional groups and intrinsic broadening of strong electron–electron scattering [11]. The corresponding value of the area under the curve is 2854.8, and FWHM is 96.85.

The I.R. spectrum (Supporting Fig. 1S) shows a broad peak at ∼ 3435 cm−1 which resembles to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (C-OH). The peaks at ∼ 1590 cm−1 and ∼ 1399 cm−1 resemble to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate anions (C-O-C), respectively. These results confirm that hydroxyl and carboxylate groups are present on the surface of CDs. Fig. 1c shows the first weight loss stage below 250C° due to the desorption of water molecules, and other stages below 400C° correspond to carbon. The moisture percent in the water desorption stage was 19.6%. The XRD patterns of the CDs (Fig. 2 a) showed a broad peak at ∼ 23°, which can be ascribed to disordered carbon atoms. The crystallite size of CDs (0.3 nm) can be calculated using Debye-Scherer’s formula (Eq. (2). Where β is full width at half maximum, θ is an angle, and λ is the wavelength of x-rays used.
(2)
d = K λ /
β c o s θ

The morphology and size of CDs were shown by FESEM and TEM studies. FESEM image shows the spherical shaped CDs particles with an average particle size of ∼ 22 nm (Fig. 2b & 2c). TEM stud confirms the formation of spherical-shaped particles with a uniform dispersion of CDs without apparent aggregation. The energy dispersive studies (EDS) confirm the appearance of carbon by indicating the C-O group of the elements (Supporting Fig. 2S).

The electrochemical performances of the bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and CDs/GCE were tested by CV and DPV using 0.5 mM A.A. in 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.6) with a certain amount of A.A. solution added sequentially. As can be seen from Fig. 3 a, compared with bare GCE (0 A.A.), the peak current of CDs/GCE (from 20 to 120 µl A.A.) increased obviously. The surface structure of CDs may accelerate the redox system's electron transfer rate and increase the adsorption of A.A. at the sensor interface. DPV measurements were also conducted to confirm the performance of the modified electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.6) containing 0.5 mM A.A. Fig. 3b shows DPV response of bare GCE and CQDs/GCE. We observed that a broad oxidation peak was appeared with CDs/GCE from 20 to 120 µl A.A. compared to the bare GCE. Our results indicate that CDs/GCE shows an electrocatalytic oxidation reaction for the detection of ascorbic acid. Note that the oxidation peak of A.A. existed positive transfer with increase of A.A. concentration. This behaviour could be related with the change of the pH resulting from the accumulation of the oxidation of A.A. Electrocatalytic oxidation reaction of A.A. is shown as A.A. → dehydro-AA + 2H+ + 2e- in reaction Scheme 1 . Table for comparison of present work with literature is shown as Supporting Table 2S.

Hydrothermal method was successfully used to synthesize CDs at 180 °C. The structural characterization using XRD determined a significant broad peak at ∼ 23° ascribed to disordered carbon atoms with a size of ∼ 0.3 nm as estimated from the Debye–Scherrer relation. The morphological characterization using TEM and SEM showed spherical particles with uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation. The prepared CDs were successfully used to detect ascorbic acid and can potentially be adapted to chemical sensing applications. The sensor based on CDs was successfully constructed.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank to Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2023R253), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134992.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
